description: Architectural patterns for building inventory systems, item databases, and equipment in UE5

entries.title: Pattern: Inventory System
entries.description: How to structure an inventory system using a component for storage, DataAssets for item definitions, and delegates for UI updates. Keeps item data separate from inventory logic.
entries.whatItIs: A clean architecture for inventory: items are defined as data (not actors), inventory is a component that holds references to that data, and the UI listens to inventory changes through delegates.
whenToUse: RPGs, survival games, looter shooters, or any game where players collect and manage items
When items have shared properties (name, icon, weight, rarity) but different behaviors
When you need inventory on multiple actor types (player, storage chest, shop NPC)
whenNotToUse: Games with no item collection (pure platformers, racing games)
If you only have 2-3 fixed items — a simple array on the character is fine, no need for a full system
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Define items as UPrimaryDataAsset (or UDataAsset). Each item is a data-only asset: Name, Description, Icon, Weight, MaxStack, ItemType enum, and any type-specific data.
STEP 2 — Create an FInventorySlot struct: holds a reference to the item DataAsset + a stack count. This is what the inventory actually stores.
STEP 3 — Create a UInventoryComponent (inherits UActorComponent). It owns a TArray<FInventorySlot> and exposes functions: AddItem, RemoveItem, HasItem, GetItemCount.
STEP 4 — The InventoryComponent broadcasts delegates: OnInventoryChanged, OnItemAdded, OnItemRemoved. Never let external code modify the array directly.
STEP 5 — For the UI: create an inventory widget that binds to OnInventoryChanged. When the delegate fires, the widget rebuilds its grid/list. The widget reads from the component, never writes.
STEP 6 — For item usage/equipping: create a separate system that reads from the inventory and acts on item data. Don't put equip logic inside the inventory component.
STEP 7 — For persistence: serialize the inventory as an array of {ItemID, Count} pairs. Load item DataAssets by ID through the AssetManager.
commonMistakes: Making items as Actors that exist in the world while in inventory — items in inventory should be data only, spawn an Actor only for world pickups
Storing item properties (damage, heal amount) in the inventory instead of on the DataAsset — the inventory only holds references and counts
Coupling the inventory to a specific Actor type — keep it as a generic component
Having the UI directly modify the inventory array — always go through AddItem/RemoveItem so delegates fire and validation happens
Not separating item definition from item instance — a 'Sword' DataAsset defines what a sword IS, an inventory slot says you HAVE one
Using DataTables for items when DataAssets would be cleaner — DataAssets are individual assets that can reference other assets (meshes, icons), DataTables are better for flat numeric data
goodPractices: Item definitions are DataAssets — designers create them in the editor, no code needed to add new items
InventoryComponent is generic — works on players, chests, NPCs, anything
All mutations go through the component's public API — never expose the internal array
Use an ItemType enum (Weapon, Consumable, Material, Quest) to drive UI display and usage logic
For multiplayer: the server owns the inventory, clients see a replicated copy
Keep the world pickup Actor (APickupActor) separate — it has a mesh and collision, and when interacted with, it calls InventoryComponent->AddItem and destroys itself
